Physical Address: Polymer Science Program, Department of Chemistry, University of Massachusetts Lowell, One University Avenue, Lowell, Massachusetts 01854, USA
Evidence Text: LCCC was conducted on a Waters HPLC system with Waters 2695 separation module at a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min. For UV/RI system, a Waters 2414 Refractive Index detector at 30[◦] C was used along with a Waters 2996 Photodiode Array detector operating at A = 254 nm. 10 we L samples of 0.5 wt% polymer solutions were injected for UV/RI system. All measurements were carried out at a constant column temperature of 35[◦] C and constant sample temperature of 30[◦] C. One reversed phase C18 chromatography column (YMC HPLC COLUMN) with average particle size = 5 we m, pore diameters = 120 A i][˚] and column size (Inner diameter × Length) = 4.6 mm × 250 mm was used. HPLC grade THF and methanol were used to prepare the mobile phase and the solution of the polymers. The solvents were freshly distilled before use to avoid any degassing related issue. The critical solvent composition for PIB was established as THF/methanol 80.5/19.5 (w/w). Premixing of the mobile phase by weight is necessary for a constant and exact composition. Samples were prepared by dissolving polymer in THF/methanol 80.5/19.5 (w/w).

Evidence Text: The CAP of PS was realised in a DMF–THF mobile phase on a RP octadecyl column [14,47]. This allowed us to extend the molar mass range up to the hundred thousands. As demonstrated in Fig. 1a, PS calibration standards adsorb in 100% DMF on a silica-based octadecyl column. The higher molar mass standards elute later than the lower molar mass standards. The elution volume decreases as the amount of THF in the mobile phase is increased. A point is reached at which all PS standards elute at more or less the same elution volume irrespective of their molar masses (THF–DMF: 18–82). These conditions are called the critical conditions or the CAP of PS for the specific column, temperature and mobile phase conditions used.

Physical Address: H.E.J. Research Institute of Chemistry, International Centre for Chemical and Biological Sciences (ICCBS), University of Karachi, Karachi, Pakistan
Evidence Text: The CAP of PEO was realised on a normal phase Si column in the same mobile phase system (THF–DMF) which is used for the critical conditions of PS. THF is an adsorption promoting solvent for PEOs on a normal phase Si column. The addition of DMF promotes desorption. At a mobile phase composition of 96–4 (THF–DMF, vol.%), all PEO standards elute at more or less the same elution volume and show no molar mass dependence. Further increase in the DMF content in the mobile phase leads to the exclusion of PEOs from pores of the stationary phase resulting in earlier elution of higher molar mass standards than the lower molar mass standards (Fig. 10a).

Physical Address: H.E.J. Research Institute of Chemistry, International Centre for Chemical and Biological Sciences (ICCBS), University of Karachi, Karachi, Pakistan
Evidence Text: Liquid chromatography at the critical conditions of PS allows the molar mass independent elution of PS homopolymers and the separation of the PS-b-PEO block copolymers which is governed by the PEO block irrespective of the size of the PS block. These conditions are very useful for the analysis of complex polymer mixtures. In our previous study under critical conditions of PS for PS-b-PEO copolymers, only low molar mass samples could be analysed [32]. In the current study, the critical conditions for PS were established using a different solvent combination in order to extend the molar mass range.

Evidence Text: The CAP of PS was realised in a DMF–THF mobile phase on a RP octadecyl column [14,47]. This allowed us to extend the molar mass range up to the hundred thousands. As demonstrated in Fig. 1a, PS calibration standards adsorb in 100% DMF on a silica-based octadecyl column. The higher molar mass standards elute later than the lower molar mass standards. The elution volume decreases as the amount of THF in the mobile phase is increased. A point is reached at which all PS standards elute at more or less the same elution volume irrespective of their molar masses (THF–DMF: 18–82). These conditions are called the critical conditions or the CAP of PS for the specific column, temperature and mobile phase conditions used.

Physical Address: H.E.J. Research Institute of Chemistry, International Centre for Chemical and Biological Sciences (ICCBS), University of Karachi, Karachi, Pakistan
Evidence Text: The CAP of PEO was realised on a normal phase Si column in the same mobile phase system (THF–DMF) which is used for the critical conditions of PS. THF is an adsorption promoting solvent for PEOs on a normal phase Si column. The addition of DMF promotes desorption. At a mobile phase composition of 96–4 (THF–DMF, vol.%), all PEO standards elute at more or less the same elution volume and show no molar mass dependence. Further increase in the DMF content in the mobile phase leads to the exclusion of PEOs from pores of the stationary phase resulting in earlier elution of higher molar mass standards than the lower molar mass standards (Fig. 10a).

Evidence Text: The simulations show that, in agreement with recent experimental results, the B blocks have a small, but noticeable, impact on the overall elution of the graft copolymer. This influence exists even when the chains are modeled as RWs, indicating that its fundamental origin is due to chain connectivity, not excluded volume interactions. In general, both models show that grafting B blocks to an A backbone in SEC mode tends to increase copolymer retention, while grafting B blocks to an A backbone in LAC mode tends to decrease copolymer retention.
